data.title: Planet Fitness: Too Heavy Debt And Valuation
data.description:
Intro

Before we do the heavy lifting on the fundamentals, a brief overview of one of the largest fitness chains on the planet.

Planet Fitness (PLNT) is not only one of the largest, but also the fastest-growing franchisors and operators of fitness clubs. The company will soon have 3,000+ studios (2,896 per the latest update) with around 21 million members. PLNT owns and self-operates ~10% of the clubs. The remainder is franchised and third-party managed.

Long-term, shares have been on the rise, being up almost 400% since being publicly listed.

Besides the healthier-living lifestyle adoption, especially the weight-loss wave seems to be a meaningful tailwind for the business. People who take the prominent weight-loss medications need to take care of their muscle mass—be it at companies like Planet Fitness.

Even though I like this thesis, I do not like the stock at the current setup. It is too expensive in my view, while the debt load adds more to my concerns.

The stock does not look that fit

Planet Fitness has strong recognition value. The colors purple and yellow, as well as its self-proclaimed mission to welcome everyone free of judgment, regardless of any attributes and fitness level, define the company.

The entry level membership starts at $15/month, offering a low entry hurdle for a membership sign-up. More ambitious gym visitors can choose a black card (to be raised from 24.99 USD to 29.99 USD), offering further add-ons, among others massage chairs, exclusive training areas, etc.

A bit surprisingly for me, PLNT doesn’t offer (from their perspective) non-essentials such as group exercise classes, pools, or juice bars. The reason is they focus on using available space as efficiently as possible. I said, surprisingly for me, because here in Germany—not necessarily the benchmark, but that’s what I have in front of me to compare—even lower-cost gyms do offer at least some sort of exercise classes. Even if only with a monitor, this form of training has risen in popularity, among others due to its motivational factor (think of “together we are strong”).

Be it, Planet Fitness should turn more and more into a less capital-intensive business. Franchising is known for being high margin, as the franchisee is responsible for operating the business—including day-to-day costs. For around 90% of their branded studios, they collect royalties for licensing. A quick look at Capex reveals that it has been broadly stable over the recent quarters—despite the company being in heavy expansion mode, as can be seen by rising sales.

Management is currently not paying dividends, but investing in the business, while free cash flow is used on an opportunistic basis to repurchase shares.

There were even a few quarters where management spent more on buybacks than was generated in FCF. Longer-time readers of my analyses know that I am not a big fan of such moves. Especially if the balance sheet is strongly leveraged while shares are priced at a high multiple.

Let’s check whether this is the case. Indeed, net debt is around $1.7 billion. Compared to FCF of ~$200 million, we are talking about a leverage factor of more than 8. The more commonly used net debt to EBITDA metric shows 3.3x, which sounds significantly lower, but even this figure is not low.

So PLNT, for my taste, operates with too much leverage. And this is real financial debt, without leasing.

Regarding the high-multiple question, unfortunately this, too, is the case. PLNT has an equity market cap of $7.6 billion for an enterprise value of around $9.3 billion. Compared to FCF, we are talking about more than 45x.

On a price-to-earnings basis, PLNT stock is valued at 30x. There’s a noticeable gap between net income and FCF, but whichever figure we use, both are high multiples, having priced in solid growth.

So, let’s discuss growth to get a better feel for the static multiples from above.

On their recently held Investor Day, Planet Fitness showed a few interesting slides in their presentation. The total gym market as a whole is barely growing (total U.S. gym membership growth of 1% since 2019), which might be a bit surprising, given the weight-loss tailwinds.

PLNT is focusing on the “affordable mid-market” segment, so to speak (HVLP = high value, low price), benefiting and even growing market share.

What this also tells us is that cost-of-living struggles continue. Else mid-tier and above classified gyms would not be coping with shrinking demand. For me, this is a clear sign of down-trading and value-seeking behavior. In other words, this carries the risk of a reversal sometime, as these potentially “borrowed” customers someday might want to return to higher-value offers like pools or training classes.

That’s why I am a bit cautious about the current growth story. It looks more like a redistribution than a full-blown growth story when looking at the sector.

After the lockdown years, sales growth almost collapsed into only low-single digits. For a bit more than a year, sales growth dynamics have accelerated again for Planet Fitness, likely as a result of what I described above. In a market that is barely growing, we should be more cautious about extrapolating such strong figures into the future.

However, the market is expecting exactly that. PLNT stock is priced for growth in a practically not-growing market. If we subtract inflation from the tiny 1% segment growth, real growth is negative. Despite weight-loss dynamics.

This could change, of course, but I would not ignore the cost component.

I rate PLNT a sell, as the stock is too richly valued, waiting for a better entry point.

The upcoming FY25 result on 24 February 2026 will likely not bring any big surprises regarding the closed fiscal year 2025. But I’d look closely at the guidance (growth momentum) and, if commented on, the Q1 26 dynamic, especially regarding the price hike for the Black Card (25% more expensive). Analysts could ask on the call about churn and pricing sensitivity.

Risks to My Thesis

The weight-loss theme could produce enduring demand for gyms, especially if combined with low prices but high value, facing the ongoing cost of living struggles.

Planet Fitness, with a market cap of less than $8 billion, could be acquired, invalidating my sell rating.

If management is able to keep pace, expanding strongly while delivering mid-double-digit sales growth, the current valuation might be less expensive than I am viewing it.

Conclusion

PLNT Fitness looks like a promising idea to play the weight-loss theme that maybe not everyone has on their radar (focus is on the drugs). However, high debt and valuation multiples force them to stay sidelined.

data.title: PayPal's Q4 Shock: Collapse Narrative Vs. Earnings Reality
data.description:
All the bad news possible in PayPal Holdings (PYPL) seems to be already in, after a torrid Q4. Leadership changes, a sharp slowdown in branded checkout growth, lower near-term guidance, and the withdrawal of prior long-term targets have led to a sharp decline in share prices. It does look like there is no room for further negatives. With shares now trading at 7-8x forward earnings, a classic contrarian opportunity emerges - expectations reset, valuation compressed, and a turnaround speculation probabilistically more supported.

However, this contrarian setup could be quite crowded, as value picks with turnaround potential are rare in today's growth markets, especially for a quality franchise like PayPal. So, it is important to argue from the other side to see whether what looks obviously cheap indeed comes with a genuine turnaround merit or whether that contrarian position simply assumes mean reversion because the stock has fallen enough.

In this thesis I do not assume that because a slower or flat earnings growth recalibration looks like expectations have been reset aggressively, it is necessarily a bottom. The thesis looks at whether current assumptions have decisive strength in expecting an EPS bottom here, and what the implications of further contractions and their odds are.

Long-term take rate durability (the percentage PayPal keeps from every transaction), volumes, and earnings transmission are all not immune from further fall at today's level. However, I find it harder work to justify a further decline than stabilization (if not a rebound). And that conservative base case of an absence of growth is already in the valuations today. So, overall, the downside risks appear lower in magnitude and odds than a turnaround from here. A measured Buy in PayPal appears to be more fundamentally supported on the basis of evidence available.

What Negatives Have Emerged?

Before analyzing the impact of the negatives, let us first understand the observable and implied problems that plague PayPal - incremental pressures that have emerged from Q4 2025 alone. I found only three real measurable negatives. One, branded checkout was reported as materially down from 5% to 1% - a significant 4 percentage point decline, pushing it to almost flat territory. The second factor is a slower exit and a lower guide into 2026. Branded checkout is guided as only slightly positive to low single digits. A 3 point transaction margin impact from investments is also expected, supporting a flat to slightly negative EPS outlook. The third negative development is a withdrawal of the multi-year long term targets shared earlier, signaling reduced visibility.

What Q4 Numbers Show - Take Rate Base Case

The impact of investments is reflected in numbers reported in Q4 (although this could be an open area to monitor), and visibility is a long duration problem to be solved in the valuation section. The remaining and only operational negative already at play is the branded checkout slowdown. What impact has it had so far? In take rate terms, the 1.65% number reported in Q4 is 9 bps lower YoY. That may seem to be a significant impact and potentially attributable to the branded checkout slowdown, but not conclusively so, if we dig a little deeper. Q4 declines reported YoY mask sequential stability in take rates - in fact, nothing declined materially in the whole of 2025. The YoY negative emerges from a stiffer comp from an outlier 4Q 2024.

In other words, the impact of branded checkout has not led to material pressures on the take rate yet. Further worsening in the mix or a higher incentive environment is required to hurt take rates ahead. Here is the problem with arguing in favor of such a decline. One, the guidance has already accepted a reset ahead and still claimed positive to low single-digit branded checkout growth in 2026. Even if it remains flat, the larger drop from 5% to 1% has had no significant effect, so further deterioration is difficult to accept as a base case.

A meaningful compression from here needs to see a permanent and material pricing reset due to incentives, or merchant leverage forcing structural fee erosion, or a further worsening of the mix. The incentives are expected to be tactical and performance-linked, and their impact on the take rate was not a huge net drag in Q4. Nothing yet indicates a permanent fee reduction, although the need to secure placements to drive engagement does show competitive pressures that should be watched. On the possibility of mix worsening, PSP and Venmo monetization trends are encouraging - areas where yields have been traditionally lower.

Actual evidence from Q4 does not indicate a trajectory that worsens fee erosion. On the contrary, PayPal's differentiated conversion data (95% vaulted success) still looks supportive of pricing power. Biometrics adoption has seen 2-5 points of conversion gains. Merchants who implemented PayPal's latest checkout features were reported to be growing at double digits.

Volumes, Revenue, EPS Implications

While branded checkout impact was a drag, there is no TPV impact noted in Q4 at all. A 6% FX-neutral growth is as good as we have seen in the past 6 quarters, if not better than most. Q4 did see strong volumes in Venmo, BNPL, PSP, Enterprise, and Tap to Pay. So, overall, the branded checkout impact is contained, and volume strength can still compensate for a lot of take rate compression if that happens ahead, while keeping revenue growth still positive. The revenue transmission trails volume growth, but that is not a new phenomenon - this can be attributed to the mix changes that have been at play throughout 2025. Q4 numbers do show a moderation in revenue transmission from volume, but nothing structurally indicates a collapse, and there is not sufficient reason yet to be able to confidently model a lower revenue transmission ahead. This could be a watchlist item at best.

Finally, the EPS trajectory does show a visible decline from Q2 2025, but still remains positive in terms of YoY growth, so it is indeed a trend that needs to be watched, but has not collapsed to historical lows (like its valuations have). There are two routes open for further deterioration in EPS ahead. One, a larger-than-expected 3-point TM headwind (assuming Q4 effects from these headwinds were absent, which is not the case). And two, worsening in branded checkout, which we have discussed as looking like a controlled risk at the moment.

Valuations, Visibility, and Residual Risks

We started the thesis with the negatives at play that have emerged in Q4. After analyzing the take rate and volume, EPS transmission factors, I am left with two residual risks that need to be monitored: not enough data to indicate a base case of further decline. Those two risks are competitive pressures (and value creation at checkout) and ongoing deterioration in revenue and EPS transmission (including impact from investments).

The negative we haven't addressed yet is lower visibility (due to far year targets withdrawal). Here, the immediate next year's guidance does not point to anything severely crushing that jumps out in 2026. In fact, EPS outlook is a low single-digit decline to slightly positive. And this poor visibility or a slower EPS road ahead is already priced in - Seeking Alpha analyst estimates show a flat 2026 and only ~10% EPS growth in 2027. PayPal has to deliver a declining EPS regime in 2026 and a flat 2027 outlook to really open up further downside risks, because the valuation could still absorb flat to low single-digit negative EPS growth territory, having corrected meaningfully from the 2025 highs (almost ~50% from the peak valuations).

Valuations have corrected by over 20% just after Q4. The EPS revisions in the past one month (which includes the post-Q4 effect) have been mid- to high single-digit down from earlier estimates. While that is a material downgrade, the forward PE ratio has shrunk by a far larger degree, indicating that the share price has reacted more severely than the forward year earnings downgrade.

That correction could still be fair, because outer-year and long-duration growth revisions also show a cascading effect that reduces terminal value. This could be a result of poorer visibility ahead, or could be a mispriced overreaction. The only case that is difficult to assign high odds to is that earnings will deteriorate further from the expectation reset we had in Q4 (which is what emerges from my analysis thus far). And even if that happens, valuations currently could support some earnings shocks if the structural competitive advantages at checkout and the turnaround initiatives demonstrate credible long-term durability from here.

So, overall, based on current resilience and data (Q4), I think PayPal can be considered a contrarian buy with risks to monitor. The downside risks appear far lower, while a successful phase of investment-driven gains could indeed open up significant upside from a valuation rebound, plus the earnings improvement.

data.title: CEF Market Review: The Double-Whammy Of 'Juicy Yield' CEFs
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual fund news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the second week of February. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferred/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

It was a good week for CEFs as NAVs were higher across all sectors. MLPs and Utilities finished ahead. Discounts were mixed.

The median CEF sector discount has tightened to around 2% below its longer-term average level, which marks the sector as fairly expensive for now.

Market Themes

John Hancock CEFs released quarterly financials. These mostly preferred funds HPI, HPF and HPS tend to trade at a premium to the sector because of their high distribution rates.

Their NAV distribution rates are around 2% above the sector median levels. However, the financials confirm that these high distribution rates are not backed up by income. They have low distribution coverage of sub-80%.

The funds underperformed the sector over the past year, in part because of underperformance in total NAV returns as well as high starting valuations, which remain high. 3-year valuation-adjusted total NAV returns are the lowest in the sector apart from NPFD. The funds have a large minority corporate bond allocation, so they are less compelling as preferred assets or from a qualified-tax perspective.

They also highlight the double whammy with chasing "juicy yield" CEFs with no regard to income. Because these funds tend to trade at more expensive valuations, investors are actually earning less in net income than they would in a similar fund with a lower distribution rate. And two, a high starting valuation limits upside in the fund and increases the downside in case of a distribution rightsizing.

Market Commentary

CLO equity NAVs are starting to come out for January. ECC was down 3.7%. EIC was down very marginally for a 0.7% total NAV return in January. EIC is the only fund in the sector that generated a positive total NAV return last year. It was not all that exciting; however, the fund got punished just as much as the rest of the suite. In short, the market is not differentiating EIC from the rest of the sector despite its significantly lower CLO equity allocation, which means it remains an opportunity. EIC trades at a 22% discount.

TCW Strategic Income Fund (TSI) announced a rights offering. The offering will be 1:3 with the record date being 19-Feb, expiring on 18-Mar. The formula will be a 7.5% discount to the price, floored at a 12% discount to NAV. The rights are transferable and will start to trade on 18 Feb under the ticker TSI RT.

TSI also announced it raised the distribution by 31% to $0.283. The latest report shows it earns around $0.025 in NII. The jump in the distribution was likely intended to prevent an excessive drop in the price on the announcement of a rights offering. The stock fell around 5% at the open yesterday, though it clawed back around half of this, leaving the fund down 2.3% on the day. TSI is an unusual fund with a mostly MBS allocation and no leverage. Performance has been unexciting.

BlackRock completed the reorganization of some of its Muni CEFs (BFZ) into MUC, BNY, MHN into MYN and MUE, BFK, BYM, BLE into MHD). The surviving funds will adopt a discount management program. Specifically, if they trade at an average discount of more than 10% during a 9-month period, they will conduct a tender offer of 5% of shares at a 2% discount. The measurement period will run to 30 Sep. That's a disappointingly long measurement period. Previously these periods ran for 3 months for BlackRock CEFs. Plus, the discount trigger used to be 7.5% rather than 10%. Now that the surviving funds are significantly larger, they are less vulnerable to an activist takeover, so BlackRock doesn't have to try as hard to be shareholder friendly.

data.title: GRAIL: A Major Trial Disappointment
data.description:
GRAIL, Inc. (GRAL) reported the extensive NHS-Galleri trial’s results alongside the company’s Q4 results. The trial results weren’t as good as was hoped; the stock has reacted very strongly due to the lack of statistically significant findings of clinical utility, being down by -50% following the study results as of writing. The trial came with major importance, in my opinion making a strong stock reaction justified.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous December 2025 article on the stock, titled “GRAIL: Making Strides In Cancer Detection.” The stock has since lost --43% of its value. The S&P 500 has lost -1% in the same period.

NHS-Galleri Results Were a Disappointment

GRAIL announced topline results of the extensive three-year NHS-Galleri trial, which used a total of 142 thousand participants aged 50 to 77. The U.K. trial is the largest, and only randomized controlled trial of any MCED test, making its results very important. Unfortunately, the trial’s key findings didn’t meet expectations. After PATHFINDER 2 previously found good results for Galleri, as I wrote in my previous December article, NHS-Galleri now suggests weaker conclusions of current clinical utility for Galleri with a multiple times larger participant group and a highly valuable randomized controlled trial setting.

The key headline is that the trial did not observe a statistically significant reduction in Stage III-IV cancers among participants. This key headline is critical regarding clinical utility—not finding statistically significant evidence is a substantial disappointment in such an extensive clinical trial, especially as Galleri’s PATHFINDER 2 study set quite good expectations for Galleri's predictive value. GRAIL’s stock’s previous run-up, and crash following the NHS-Galleri topline results, underline the market’s disappointment.

GRAIL plans to extend the trial’s follow-up period by 6-12 months. A longer follow-up period could improve study results’ reliability as data matures, but for now, initial topline results are weaker than was hoped.

The trial did find a favorable trend towards fewer Stage III-IV cancers in 12 different cancers, a similar positive predictive value “PPV” to GRAIL’s North American studies, and some other positive results in cancer detection, according to GRAIL’s press release. Overall results were still disappointingly inconclusive due to the lack of statistical significance in the most important endpoint.

Ultimately, the key question is how significant the trial results are for GRAIL’s commercialization. The stock’s sharp reaction suggests that the implications are massive.

I also believe that the NHS-Galleri trial holds very material importance for GRAIL. The NHS-Galleri trial was set to explore whether Galleri could be adopted for a national cancer screening program within the NHS. A national healthcare program with the U.K.’s nearly 70 million population could create massive revenue potential for GRAIL depending on the program’s patient reach. I believe that inconclusive trial results make an extensive commercial program with the NHS much more unlikely.

Implications reach past the U.K. as well. GRAIL completed its application for Galleri’s FDA premarket approval in January. The approval process uses data from PATHFINDER 2, but also from first-year data from the NHS-Galleri trial. FDA approval will more than likely be critical to gain commercial mass in the U.S. market, and NHS-Galleri’s failure to meet the primary endpoint could affect approval. The review process is anticipated by GRAIL to take around 12 months.

Fundamentally, the inconclusive trial results from the most extensive clinical trial on MCEDs make GRAIL’s value proposition more uncertain. Galleri is much more unlikely to gain insurance coverage with uncertain proof of clinical utility. Additional trials will need to come up with more conclusive results to prove clinical utility; the focus shifts towards GRAIL's REACH study, aiming to study 50 thousand participants in the Medicare population.

While the key endpoint result of NHS-Galleri is clearly worse than anticipated, I believe that the trial results don’t deteriorate the whole Galleri story. The study did still show some positive results regarding clinical utility. A prolonged follow-up period could strengthen the trial’s findings. More detailed NHS-Galleri trial results are going to be presented in mid-2026. For now, the results do make the overall Galleri story much more uncertain, justifying a sharp stock reaction.

GRAIL’s Financials Performed as Expected in Q4

GRAIL's Q4 financials were close to expectations. Revenues came in at $43.6 million, up by 14% year-on-year. The Galleri test’s revenues improved by 31% to $41.3 million, being the most important revenue stream to watch. The adjusted EBITDA loss came in at -$71.8 million, consistent with previous quarters in 2025 but improving by $12.2 million year-on-year. GRAIL is still largely in the development phase; the revenue base is way too small to generate positive earnings yet.

Revenues were very close to Wall Street's consensus, and the quarter’s EPS of -$2.44 even beat Wall Street’s expectations by $0.22.

GRAIL’s partnership with Hims & Hers (HIMS) among other commercial expansions, creates revenue momentum for 2026 as well. GRAIL guides Galleri to show sales growth of 22-32% for the year, and that the company’s cash burn will be no more than $300 million. GRAIL ended the quarter with $904 million in combined cash and short-term marketable securities, enabling the company to burn cash for the next few years.

Ultimately, while growth momentum is positive, the focus remains on FDA approval and other meaningful regulatory progress that would enable fast commercial adoption. Current financials don’t have very much significance in my opinion past the cash burn rate.

Valuation

Using my previous assumptions of $900 million in operating expenses upon maturation, a 55% gross margin, and a justifiable mature P/E of 20, and adding a 25% tax rate, GRAIL’s stock price of $50.6 as of writing implies revenues of around $1.88 billion upon maturation. GRAIL’s revenue potential is multiple times larger if clinical utility is proven extensively, but even reaching such a revenue figure is still on uncertain grounds.

For now, I believe that such a valuation doesn’t seem too far off from a fair base valuation that incorporates multiple potential financial scenarios.

The valuation remains very volatile; a wide range of stock prices can be justified with such calculations. The calculation reflects a number of highly volatile assumptions regarding operating expenses, revenues, and earnings multiples, and only works as a very rough illustration of potential stock pricing.

Takeaway

NHS-Galleri results weren’t satisfactory, as GRAIL didn’t prove the main endpoint with statistical significance. The trial results question Galleri’s clinical utility, weaken prospects for an extensive national program with the NHS, and cast a slight shadow on the FDA approval process; a negative stock reaction was justified. The investment case is now much more uncertain, but ultimately, I don’t expect the trial results to deteriorate the whole Galleri story. I remain at a Hold rating.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - February 2026
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. The regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. The emphasis here is on high growth rather than high yield. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P 500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who Should Follow This Series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace than in their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook

Overall, the market appears to be somewhat tired and stalled. Even though the market had a reasonably good start in the new year, with the S&P 500 advancing 1.4% in the month of January. We have already seen the S&P 500 make new all-time highs four times this year, and it also briefly crossed the psychologically important level of SPX 7000 but retreated quickly afterward.

During this consolidation phase, some new trends that started in 2025 are picking up steam. For example, the foreign equities are outperforming the U.S. equities, which started in 2025. Also, the mid-cap and small-cap stocks are expected to outperform the large-cap stocks.

The big question is if the AI rally of the past year is in bubble territory and ready to burst. It is hard to predict, and even if it was, bubbles can last longer than most predictions. That said, there is a growing concern about overspending and over-borrowing by the mega technology companies. With this backdrop, as investors, we need to be cautious.

Another factor that is going to impact the markets and the economy is the Fed's actions on interest rates in 2026. It will be pivotal in shaping the broader economy and the market. The Fed stayed pat and kept the interest rates on hold (target rate between 3.50% and 3.75%) during their most recent FOMC meeting on 27/28th January. However, with the most recent inflation numbers, there are now higher expectations of a rate cut in June 2026.

You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatch Tool website.

All that being said, we can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. We intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

Selection Criteria for High-Growth Dividend Stocks:

Note: For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article). This section is common among articles under this series. The regular readers could skip this section.

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth)
  • Payout ratio (dividend per share divided by EPS)
  • Past three years and five-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/capital and debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or revenue growth during the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95%, but preferably less than 80%.

  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 3-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score".

Narrowing Down the List to 20 Stocks:

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry overconcentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 3-year dividend growth. This is to account for more recent growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years)

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (3 years)

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with the Highest EPS and RS Rating

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: APH, AU, COKE, FIX, HWM, TRGP, and WWD (7 duplicates).

Appeared three times: CIB (2 duplicates).

Appeared four times: VRT (3 duplicates).

We are now left with 38 (50 - 12) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is one on the list. We remove GE. There are also two others that don't have the sales growth figures readily available, but we will give them the benefit of the doubt and keep them for now. Now, we have 37 names left.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes WIT, AXIA, PGR, and MSFT. We now have 33 (37-4) names.

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. We remove AEG. We still have 32 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 60. There are three such names. We remove CI and EQT. We are now left with 30 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at our wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is 'not' a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative Strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. This month, the turnover is quite high, as six (out of ten) stocks are being replaced.

If a stock from the previous month has been dropped or replaced, it does not mean that the stock is no longer attractive. It simply means that our filtering process has found something more appealing in terms of momentum if two stocks are at par qualitatively.
